what_it_does: Renders a proposal consent surface in a React app, switching between a one-click option list for safe verbs (B1) and a side-by-side comparison with visual-only selection plus a separate approve-and-build commit for consequential interactive verbs (B2); also provides steer/refine, defer, set-aside, and dismiss channels.
observations: Mode is chosen by the boolean proposal.interactive, not by the number of options.
For B2, clicking a comparison card only toggles local selectedIdx; no backend call happens until the explicit approve button is pressed.
If proposal.options is missing or empty, the component synthesizes a single option from the top-level proposal fields.
Local steer text and selectedIdx are reset via useEffect whenever the proposal identity changes.
The steer/refine input is disabled while chatBusy and submits on Enter.
Both B1 and B2 surfaces expose defer (queue to inbox), setAside (drop without keeping), and dismiss (reject) actions.
No data-ui-ref attributes are used on the interactive comparison cards; they are treated as ephemeral affordances.
The direction/steer channel can be disabled when proposal.direction === false.
standouts: B2 select-then-approve invariant: selection is visual-only and a distinct approve button is the only control that runs /api/act.
B1 one-click commit for non-interactive safe verbs.
Steer loop sends operator refinement back to the RHM and expects a new proposal offer in return.
Defer queues the live offer to an inbox for later revival.
conventions: React functional component using useState, useEffect, and a context hook for actions and data.
UI-only state is kept local; proposal data and action handlers live in AppContext.
Uses kit primitives Surface and Badge with tone props (sig, dim, await) for visual semantics.
Inline comments encode design rationale and policy rules (B1/B2/B3) alongside the code.
Conditional CSS class names are built with string concatenation.
Guard clause returns null when no proposal is present.
concerns: selectedIdx is reset only on proposal identity change, so a mutated options array without a new proposal reference could leave a stale or out-of-bounds selection.
The approve button's onClick checks sel && approveProposal(sel), but the disabled state already prevents clicks when sel is null; the redundancy is harmless but slightly inconsistent.
No visible TypeScript interfaces or prop types for proposal/options shapes in this file.
B1 options call approveProposal immediately on click with no confirmation, which is by design but assumes the backend correctly classifies safe verbs.
Title attributes carry significant explanatory copy; they may be less accessible than explicit ARIA labels.
notes: A policy-aware consent widget that makes the distinction between safe one-click actions and consequential builds explicit in the UI.
questions: What is the exact runtime shape of proposal and its options entries?
How does the backend decide proposal.interactive, and what prevents a consequential verb from being offered in non-interactive mode?
What do approveProposal, deferProposal, setAsideProposal, and dismissProposal do under the hood, and do they return promises?
How is the inbox/defer queue persisted and revived?
Is there a second operator approval step after build/panel/extend, and where is it implemented?
Are there tests covering the B1/B2 mode switching and the select-then-approve invariant?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the comments describe a B1 offer-with-options surface and a B2 on-screen interactive build comparison, and the code implements both flows with the stated invariants.
apparent_themes: Graded dismissal: defer vs set-aside vs dismiss.
Policy encoded in UI structure, not just copy.
intention_signals: The file is trying to make the AI assistant's offers feel like a negotiated consent moment: low-friction for safe actions, but a guided comparison with a separate commit step for anything that could materially change the system.
novelty: A single component that conditionally renders either a one-click action menu or a 'compare alternatives, discuss, then approve' surface based on a backend-supplied interactive flag, with a steer loop that treats refinement as a new proposal.
contribution: It contributes a reusable, mode-aware proposal surface that encodes the product's consent policy directly in its layout and control flow, including visual-only selection, explicit approval, and steer/defer/dismiss channels.
summary_for_embedding: ProposeAffordance.tsx is a React region component that renders an AI proposal consent surface, toggling between a one-click safe-action list and a select-then-approve comparison for consequential interactive builds, with steer, defer, set-aside, and dismiss controls.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to implement a two-mode consent model where safe verbs commit in one click but consequential build/panel/extend verbs require side-by-side comparison, discussion, and an explicit separate approval before running.
suggested_fields: consent_mode_policy to record whether a surface is B1 or B2.
action_consequence_level to tag safe vs interactive/consequential verbs.
backend_proposal_schema to store the expected proposal/options shape.

symbols.does: React component that renders the proposal affordance surface.
symbols.description: Mounts the B1/B2 consent UI based on proposal.interactive, manages local selection and steer text, and wires user choices to AppContext action handlers.

symbols.does: Sends a non-empty steer message to the RHM.
symbols.description: Trims the local steer input, clears it, and calls steerProposal to loop the operator's refinement back as a new proposal offer.
